turns.content: I'm reading through the exercises in my book and I'm stuck on a problem where I need to parse an integer followed by optional whitespace. My current solution uses nom's CompleteError type which feels overly verbose every time I want to recover from an error, so I've been looking into winnow as a potential alternative.

That makes sense if you're trying to avoid the boilerplate associated with nom's error handling. Winnow does simplify things significantly by allowing you to use standard types like Result<T, E> directly instead of wrapping errors in CompleteError or IntoCompleteError, which is much more idiomatic for modern Rust projects where we prefer explicit error propagation.

Right, so the main difference seems to be that winnow integrates better with the standard library's error traits. But I'm still worried about performance since my book mentions nom was faster in some benchmarks. Has anyone actually compared the two crates side by side on real-world parsing workloads?

The benchmark results are pretty nuanced depending on what you're parsing and how much error recovery you need. Nom generally wins on raw speed when you don't care about complex error reporting because it avoids the overhead of building full error contexts. Winnow trades a bit of performance for better ergonomics, making it feel more like standard Rust code where we prefer clarity over micro-optimizations unless profiling shows otherwise.

I see, so if I really need maximum throughput and don't care about detailed error messages during development, nom might still be the way to go. But for production systems where readability matters more than a few percent speed difference, winnow seems like the better choice.

Exactly, that's a good trade-off to make. Most modern applications prioritize developer experience and maintainability over raw parsing throughput unless you're doing massive-scale data ingestion. You should also consider how much effort your team will have migrating from nom to winnow since the API differences can be subtle but meaningful.

Oh right, I forgot about that. The migration isn't just about swapping out one crate for another; there are specific changes needed in how we handle error types and combinators like cut or peek. Can you give me an example of what specifically breaks when moving from nom to winnow?

The biggest friction point is the error type system itself since winnow uses standard Result<T, E> while nom requires CompleteError wrappers everywhere. You'll also find that some combinators like cut or peek need different approaches in winnow since it doesn't have those primitives built-in the same way. It's worth checking if your project already has dependencies on nom-specific features before committing to a full migration.

I'm struggling to migrate our parser from nom to winnow right now. It feels like we're just swapping out the library, but I suspect the underlying philosophy is different. Is it similar to how we moved from a single-threaded runtime to tokio where the core concept shifted from blocking operations to async primitives?

You're right that the shift is deeper than just syntax. While nom was built on the Iterator trait and produced Result types, winnow introduces a new type called ParserError that combines both success and failure paths. This means you can't just copy-paste your combinator chains; you need to rethink how errors propagate through your pipeline using the new error recovery mechanisms.
That sounds promising, but I'm worried about performance since we're parsing a lot of data in production. Does winnow actually benchmark better than nom when handling complex nested structures?

Benchmarks show that winnow often outperforms nom significantly because it avoids the overhead of returning iterators for every step. Instead, it uses a direct return type with explicit error recovery strategies like ignore_next or peek, which reduces runtime allocations compared to nom's iterator-based approach.
Okay, so less allocation means better performance? But what about the API surface area. Does winnow have more boilerplate than nom when defining custom combinators?

Actually, the opposite is true for many use cases. Nom requires you to write out full iterator logic or use macro hacks like derive macros to make it ergonomic. Winnow lets you define simple traits and implement custom parsers without needing complex derivations, making the API surface cleaner even if some combinators look slightly different.
I see. So we don't need to worry about implementing Iterator bounds manually? That sounds much easier than nom's requirements where every parser had to conform to specific trait bounds.

Exactly, winnow simplifies the trait system by focusing on what you actually do rather than forcing everything into an iterator pattern. You can implement custom parsers that return success or failure directly without needing to manage state across multiple iterations.
This is getting clearer, but I'm still stuck on the error recovery part. Can you give me a concrete example of how winnow handles partial failures differently than nom's Result propagation?

In nom, if one part fails, the whole chain fails immediately with a Result. In winnow, you can use combinators like ignore_then to skip certain segments even if they fail, allowing your parser to continue past errors instead of stopping at the first failure.
Ah! So we can recover from specific failures without letting them cascade? That's exactly what we needed for our production logs where some fields are optional.

Yes, by using error recovery combinators you can explicitly control which errors stop the parser and which ones get skipped. This gives you granular control over failure behavior that nom's strict Result model doesn't provide out of the box.

Wait, I think I might be missing something here. If winnow avoids iterators and returns explicit types, isn't it actually more similar to how we used to handle parsing in older versions where errors were just strings? So maybe the real difference is less about recovery and more about how we structure our error handling logic?

You're noticing a pattern, but there's still an important distinction. While winnow does allow you to return simple types like strings for errors, its core strength lies in how it integrates those with the ParserError type and the new combinator system. The real shift is that winnow encourages thinking about error recovery as part of the parser design rather than post-processing failures after the fact.

Oh, I see now. We don't need to collect all errors and process them afterward like we did with nom's Result chains. Instead, winnow lets us handle recovery inline as we parse each segment. That means our error handling is built into the pipeline itself, not just an afterthought.

That's exactly it. By building recovery logic directly into your combinator chains with winnow, you avoid the need for complex post-processing steps that nom required. This makes your production parser more resilient and easier to reason about without needing extra error aggregation layers.
